passages.text: Without contraries is no progression. Attraction and repulsion, reason and energy, love and hate, are necessary to human existence.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell
passages.context: The foundational axiom of Blake's dialectical vision. Opposites are not errors to be resolved but the engine of all movement. Suppress one side and you kill the whole.

passages.text: Good is the passive that obeys reason; Evil is the active springing from Energy. Good is heaven. Evil is hell.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell
passages.context: Blake inverts the moral hierarchy. What religion names 'Evil' is simply Energy — the active principle of life. Passive obedience to reason is what gets called 'Good.' Blake refuses to accept this labeling.

passages.text: Energy is the only life, and is from the Body; and Reason is the bound or outward circumference of Energy. Energy is Eternal Delight.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell
passages.context: The Body is not inferior to the Soul — it is the source of Energy, which is life itself. Reason merely marks the boundary of Energy. And Energy is not burden but delight, eternally.

passages.text: Those who restrain desire, do so because theirs is weak enough to be restrained; and the restrainer or reason usurps its place and governs the unwilling.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell
passages.context: Blake's diagnosis of moral repression: only weak desire can be leashed. Strong desire cannot be merely 'restrained.' Those who moralize about restraint are confessing the poverty of their own energy.

passages.text: The road of excess leads to the palace of wisdom.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Proverbs of Hell
passages.context: A proverb from Hell — meaning, from infernal wisdom rather than religious caution. Moderation teaches less than extremity. You learn limits by transgressing them.

passages.text: He who desires, but acts not, breeds pestilence.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Proverbs of Hell
passages.context: Unexpressed desire does not simply go quiet — it corrupts. The failure to act on what one wants is not virtue but a form of internal poisoning.

passages.text: A fool sees not the same tree that a wise man sees.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Proverbs of Hell
passages.context: The same external object is a different world depending on who perceives it. Seeing is not passive registration — it is transformation by the seer.

passages.text: Eternity is in love with the productions of time.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Proverbs of Hell
passages.context: The infinite does not transcend the finite — it is drawn to it, loves it. The temporal world is not something to escape but something the eternal adores. A reversal of Platonic otherworldliness.

passages.text: No bird soars too high if he soars with his own wings.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Proverbs of Hell
passages.context: One's own power cannot lead one astray. The danger is borrowed authority, borrowed wings. The authentic self is not an excess but the only safe vehicle.

passages.text: The pride of the peacock is the glory of God. The lust of the goat is the bounty of God. The wrath of the lion is the wisdom of God. The nakedness of woman is the work of God.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Proverbs of Hell
passages.context: Everything condemned by conventional religion — pride, lust, wrath, nakedness — is reattributed to God. The very forces morality suppresses are divine expressions.

passages.text: The cistern contains, the fountain overflows.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Proverbs of Hell
passages.context: Containment is stasis; overflow is life. The creative and the vital cannot be stored — they must pour outward. Blake's preference is always for the fountain.

passages.text: The tigers of wrath are wiser than the horses of instruction.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Proverbs of Hell
passages.context: Fierce emotional knowledge exceeds systematic learning. The wild, passionate tiger perceives what the well-trained, obedient horse cannot. Energy knows; pedagogy merely rehearses.

passages.text: You never know what is enough unless you know what is more than enough.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Proverbs of Hell
passages.context: Sufficiency can only be known by exceeding it. You must pass through too-much to understand enough. A defense of extremity as the path to genuine measure.

passages.text: If the fool would persist in his folly he would become wise.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Proverbs of Hell
passages.context: Folly followed far enough becomes its own wisdom. The fool who stops halfway remains a fool. The one who persists discovers what the folly was really about.

passages.text: Improvement makes straight roads, but the crooked roads without Improvement are roads of Genius.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Proverbs of Hell
passages.context: Optimization and refinement produce efficient straightness. But Genius wanders, veers, finds its way by deviation. The crooked path that hasn't been 'improved' is where originality lives.

passages.text: What is now proved was once only imagined.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Proverbs of Hell
passages.context: Every established fact was once a wild mental act. Imagination is not decorative — it precedes reality. The established world is fossilized vision.

passages.text: How do you know but every bird that cuts the airy way is an immense world of delight, closed by your senses five?
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — A Memorable Fancy
passages.context: Written by a Devil on the rock with corroding fire. Each creature moves inside a world of its own immensity. The five senses close us off from the vast interior lives around us.

passages.text: If the doors of perception were cleansed everything would appear to man as it is, infinite. For man has closed himself up, till he sees all things through narrow chinks of his cavern.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell
passages.context: The world is not finite — perception is. We have contracted ourselves into cave-dwellers seeing narrow slits of light. Cleansed perception does not discover a different world; it reveals the actual one.

passages.text: This will come to pass by an improvement of sensual enjoyment. But first the notion that man has a body distinct from his soul is to be expunged.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell
passages.context: Redemption comes not through asceticism but through deepened sensory life. And that requires abolishing the mind-body split first. Pleasure is the path to the infinite, not the obstacle.

passages.text: The ancient poets animated all sensible objects with Gods or Geniuses... Till a system was formed, which some took advantage of and enslaved the vulgar by attempting to realize or abstract the mental deities from their objects. Thus began Priesthood.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell
passages.context: Religion began as living perception — gods embedded in things, in rivers and mountains. Priesthood arose when someone abstracted those gods away from the world and used them as tools of control.

passages.text: The voice of honest indignation is the voice of God, I cared not for consequences, but wrote.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Isaiah speaking
passages.context: Isaiah, dining with Blake, explains how he dared claim divine authority. Not vision, not theology — honest fury. Righteous anger that doesn't calculate consequences is itself divine speech.

passages.text: All poets believe that it does, and in ages of imagination this firm persuasion removed mountains; but many are not capable of a firm persuasion of anything.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Isaiah speaking
passages.context: Isaiah, asked whether firm persuasion makes things so, answers: in ages of imagination, yes. The failure is not of method but of commitment. Most cannot hold a firm persuasion long enough for it to take effect.

passages.text: The Poetic Genius was the first principle, and all the others merely derivative... all Gods would at last be proved to originate in ours, and to be the tributaries of the Poetic Genius.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — Ezekiel speaking
passages.context: Ezekiel explains the Israeli theological wager: the Poetic Genius is primary, all other principles derived. Every god is a tributary of the same imaginative source. Not sectarianism but a claim about the structure of mind.

passages.text: The worship of God is, honouring His gifts in other men each according to his genius, and loving the greatest men best. Those who envy or calumniate great men hate God, for there is no other God.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell — a Devil speaking
passages.context: A Devil's theology: God is not a being separate from creation but the genius inhering in each person. To honor God is to honor excellence in others. To diminish the great is to diminish divinity itself.

passages.text: One law for the lion and ox is Oppression.
passages.source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell
passages.context: The final epigram of the text. Uniform law applied to radically different natures is not justice but violence. The lion and the ox are not the same. One law for both crushes one of them.
